<!-- BEGIN COMPOUND CODEX TOOL MAP -->
## Compound Codex Tool Mapping (Claude Compatibility)

This section maps Claude Code plugin tool references to Codex behavior.
Only this block is managed automatically.

Tool mapping:
- Read: use shell reads (cat/sed) or rg
- Write: create files via shell redirection or apply_patch
- Edit/MultiEdit: use apply_patch
- Bash: use shell_command
- Grep: use rg (fallback: grep)
- Glob: use rg --files or find
- LS: use ls via shell_command
- WebFetch/WebSearch: use curl or Context7 for library docs
- AskUserQuestion/Question: ask the user in chat
- Task/Subagent/Parallel: run sequentially in main thread; use multi_tool_use.parallel for tool calls
- TodoWrite/TodoRead: use file-based todos in todos/ with file-todos skill
- Skill: open the referenced SKILL.md and follow it
- ExitPlanMode: ignore
<!-- END COMPOUND CODEX TOOL MAP -->

--- project-doc ---

# Repository Guidelines

## Project Structure & Module Organization
- `apps/` contains product apps (e.g., `apps/org`, `apps/donor`, `apps/builder`, `apps/storybook`).
- `packages/` contains shared packages like `packages/ui`, `packages/utils`, `packages/tokens`, and config.
- Shared scripts live in `scripts/`, repo config in `config/`, and tooling in `turbo.json` + `biome.jsonc`.

## Build, Test, and Development Commands
- `pnpm dev` starts the local dev experience via `./scripts/dev`.
- `pnpm build` runs `turbo build` for all packages/apps.
- `pnpm test` runs `turbo test` (Vitest in each workspace).
- `pnpm typecheck` runs `turbo typecheck`.
- `pnpm lint` / `pnpm lint:fix` run Biome checks and auto-fixes.
- `pnpm storybook` starts Storybook (`apps/storybook`).
- `pnpm test:storybook` runs Storybook tests.

## Coding Style & Naming Conventions
- Indentation: 2 spaces (see `biome.jsonc`).
- Prefer semantic design tokens and Tailwind utility variables from `packages/ui` (see `CONTRIBUTING.md`).
- File naming: use `kebab-case` for folders, `kebab-case.tsx` for components, and `*.test.ts(x)` for tests.
- Run Biome before pushing: `pnpm lint`.

## Testing Guidelines
- Testing framework: Vitest (see `vitest.config.ts` in apps/packages).
- Name tests as `*.test.ts` or `*.test.tsx` near the source module.
- Run all tests with `pnpm test`, or target a workspace with `pnpm --filter <pkg> test`.

## Commit & Pull Request Guidelines
- Commit messages follow Conventional Commits: `type(scope): subject` (example: `feat(sidebar): add collapse`).
- Commits must be GPG signed (see `CONTRIBUTING.md`).
- Branch naming: `[username]/[feature-description]`.
- PRs should include: concise summary, linked issue (if any), test notes, and screenshots for UI changes.

## Security & Configuration Tips
- Use the setup script for local SSL/hosts setup: `./scripts/setup.sh` (see `SETUP.md`).
- Keep `node` and `pnpm` aligned with `package.json` engines (`node >= 24.6.0`, `pnpm >= 10.6.4`).


## Skills
A skill is a set of local instructions to follow that is stored in a `SKILL.md` file. Below is the list of skills that can be used. Each entry includes a name, description, and file path so you can open the source for full instructions when using a specific skill.

## Compound Codex Tool Mapping (Claude Compatibility)

This section maps Claude Code plugin tool references to Codex behavior.
Only this block is managed automatically.

Tool mapping:
- Read: use shell reads (cat/sed) or rg
- Write: create files via shell redirection or apply_patch
- Edit/MultiEdit: use apply_patch
- Bash: use shell_command
- Grep: use rg (fallback: grep)
- Glob: use rg --files or find
- LS: use ls via shell_command
- WebFetch/WebSearch: use curl or Context7 for library docs
- AskUserQuestion/Question: ask the user in chat
- Task/Subagent/Parallel: run sequentially in main thread; use multi_tool_use.parallel for tool calls
- TodoWrite/TodoRead: use file-based todos in todos/ with file-todos skill
- Skill: open the referenced SKILL.md and follow it
- ExitPlanMode: ignore

--- project-doc ---

# Repository Guidelines

## Project Structure & Module Organization
- `apps/` contains product apps (e.g., `apps/org`, `apps/donor`, `apps/builder`, `apps/storybook`).
- `packages/` contains shared packages like `packages/ui`, `packages/utils`, `packages/tokens`, and config.
- Shared scripts live in `scripts/`, repo config in `config/`, and tooling in `turbo.json` + `biome.jsonc`.

## Build, Test, and Development Commands
- `pnpm dev` starts the local dev experience via `./scripts/dev`.
- `pnpm build` runs `turbo build` for all packages/apps.
- `pnpm test` runs `turbo test` (Vitest in each workspace).
- `pnpm typecheck` runs `turbo typecheck`.
- `pnpm lint` / `pnpm lint:fix` run Biome checks and auto-fixes.
- `pnpm storybook` starts Storybook (`apps/storybook`).
- `pnpm test:storybook` runs Storybook tests.

## Coding Style & Naming Conventions
- Indentation: 2 spaces (see `biome.jsonc`).
- Prefer semantic design tokens and Tailwind utility variables from `packages/ui` (see `CONTRIBUTING.md`).
- File naming: use `kebab-case` for folders, `kebab-case.tsx` for components, and `*.test.ts(x)` for tests.
- Run Biome before pushing: `pnpm lint`.

## Testing Guidelines
- Testing framework: Vitest (see `vitest.config.ts` in apps/packages).
- Name tests as `*.test.ts` or `*.test.tsx` near the source module.
- Run all tests with `pnpm test`, or target a workspace with `pnpm --filter <pkg> test`.

## Commit & Pull Request Guidelines
- Commit messages follow Conventional Commits: `type(scope): subject` (example: `feat(sidebar): add collapse`).
- Commits must be GPG signed (see `CONTRIBUTING.md`).
- Branch naming: `[username]/[feature-description]`.
- PRs should include: concise summary, linked issue (if any), test notes, and screenshots for UI changes.

## Security & Configuration Tips
- Use the setup script for local SSL/hosts setup: `./scripts/setup.sh` (see `SETUP.md`).
- Keep `node` and `pnpm` aligned with `package.json` engines (`node >= 24.6.0`, `pnpm >= 10.6.4`).


## Skills
A skill is a set of local instructions to follow that is stored in a `SKILL.md` file. Below is the list of skills that can be used. Each entry includes a name, description, and file path so you can open the source for full instructions when using a specific skill.

### How to use skills
- Discovery: The list above is the skills available in this session (name + description + file path). Skill bodies live on disk at the listed paths.
- Trigger rules: If the user names a skill (with `$SkillName` or plain text) OR the task clearly matches a skill's description shown above, you must use that skill for that turn. Multiple mentions mean use them all. Do not carry skills across turns unless re-mentioned.
- Missing/blocked: If a named skill isn't in the list or the path can't be read, say so briefly and continue with the best fallback.
- How to use a skill (progressive disclosure):
  1) After deciding to use a skill, open its `SKILL.md`. Read only enough to follow the workflow.
  2) When `SKILL.md` references relative paths (e.g., `scripts/foo.py`), resolve them relative to the skill directory listed above first, and only consider other paths if needed.
  3) If `SKILL.md` points to extra folders such as `references/`, load only the specific files needed for the request; don't bulk-load everything.
  4) If `scripts/` exist, prefer running or patching them instead of retyping large code blocks.
  5) If `assets/` or templates exist, reuse them instead of recreating from scratch.
- Coordination and sequencing:
  - If multiple skills apply, choose the minimal set that covers the request and state the order you'll use them.
  - Announce which skill(s) you're using and why (one short line). If you skip an obvious skill, say why.
- Context hygiene:
  - Keep context small: summarize long sections instead of pasting them; only load extra files when needed.
  - Avoid deep reference-chasing: prefer opening only files directly linked from `SKILL.md` unless you're blocked.
  - When variants exist (frameworks, providers, domains), pick only the relevant reference file(s) and note that choice.
- Safety and fallback: If a skill can't be applied cleanly (missing files, unclear instructions), state the issue, pick the next-best approach, and continue.

<!-- BEGIN COMPOUND CODEX TOOL MAP -->
## Compound Codex Tool Mapping (Claude Compatibility)

This section maps Claude Code plugin tool references to Codex behavior.
Only this block is managed automatically.

Tool mapping:
- Read: use shell reads (cat/sed) or rg
- Write: create files via shell redirection or apply_patch
- Edit/MultiEdit: use apply_patch
- Bash: use shell_command
- Grep: use rg (fallback: grep)
- Glob: use rg --files or find
- LS: use ls via shell_command
- WebFetch/WebSearch: use curl or Context7 for library docs
- AskUserQuestion/Question: ask the user in chat
- Task/Subagent/Parallel: run sequentially in main thread; use multi_tool_use.parallel for tool calls
- TodoWrite/TodoRead: use file-based todos in todos/ with file-todos skill
- Skill: open the referenced SKILL.md and follow it
- ExitPlanMode: ignore
<!-- END COMPOUND CODEX TOOL MAP -->

--- project-doc ---

# Repository Guidelines

## Project Structure & Module Organization
- `apps/` contains product apps (e.g., `apps/org`, `apps/donor`, `apps/builder`, `apps/storybook`).
- `packages/` contains shared packages like `packages/ui`, `packages/utils`, `packages/tokens`, and config.
- Shared scripts live in `scripts/`, repo config in `config/`, and tooling in `turbo.json` + `biome.jsonc`.

## Build, Test, and Development Commands
- `pnpm dev` starts the local dev experience via `./scripts/dev`.
- `pnpm build` runs `turbo build` for all packages/apps.
- `pnpm test` runs `turbo test` (Vitest in each workspace).
- `pnpm typecheck` runs `turbo typecheck`.
- `pnpm lint` / `pnpm lint:fix` run Biome checks and auto-fixes.
- `pnpm storybook` starts Storybook (`apps/storybook`).
- `pnpm test:storybook` runs Storybook tests.

## Coding Style & Naming Conventions
- Indentation: 2 spaces (see `biome.jsonc`).
- Prefer semantic design tokens and Tailwind utility variables from `packages/ui` (see `CONTRIBUTING.md`).
- File naming: use `kebab-case` for folders, `kebab-case.tsx` for components, and `*.test.ts(x)` for tests.
- Run Biome before pushing: `pnpm lint`.

## Testing Guidelines
- Testing framework: Vitest (see `vitest.config.ts` in apps/packages).
- Name tests as `*.test.ts` or `*.test.tsx` near the source module.
- Run all tests with `pnpm test`, or target a workspace with `pnpm --filter <pkg> test`.

## Commit & Pull Request Guidelines
- Commit messages follow Conventional Commits: `type(scope): subject` (example: `feat(sidebar): add collapse`).
- Commits must be GPG signed (see `CONTRIBUTING.md`).
- Branch naming: `[username]/[feature-description]`.
- PRs should include: concise summary, linked issue (if any), test notes, and screenshots for UI changes.

## Security & Configuration Tips
- Use the setup script for local SSL/hosts setup: `./scripts/setup.sh` (see `SETUP.md`).
- Keep `node` and `pnpm` aligned with `package.json` engines (`node >= 24.6.0`, `pnpm >= 10.6.4`).


## Skills
A skill is a set of local instructions to follow that is stored in a `SKILL.md` file. Below is the list of skills that can be used. Each entry includes a name, description, and file path so you can open the source for full instructions when using a specific skill.

- Discovery: The list above is the skills available in this session (name + description + file path). Skill bodies live on disk at the listed paths.
- Trigger rules: If the user names a skill (with `$SkillName` or plain text) OR the task clearly matches a skill's description shown above, you must use that skill for that turn. Multiple mentions mean use them all. Do not carry skills across turns unless re-mentioned.
- Missing/blocked: If a named skill isn't in the list or the path can't be read, say so briefly and continue with the best fallback.
- How to use a skill (progressive disclosure):
  1) After deciding to use a skill, open its `SKILL.md`. Read only enough to follow the workflow.
  2) When `SKILL.md` references relative paths (e.g., `scripts/foo.py`), resolve them relative to the skill directory listed above first, and only consider other paths if needed.
  3) If `SKILL.md` points to extra folders such as `references/`, load only the specific files needed for the request; don't bulk-load everything.
  4) If `scripts/` exist, prefer running or patching them instead of retyping large code blocks.
  5) If `assets/` or templates exist, reuse them instead of recreating from scratch.
- Coordination and sequencing:
  - If multiple skills apply, choose the minimal set that covers the request and state the order you'll use them.
  - Announce which skill(s) you're using and why (one short line). If you skip an obvious skill, say why.
- Context hygiene:
  - Keep context small: summarize long sections instead of pasting them; only load extra files when needed.
  - Avoid deep reference-chasing: prefer opening only files directly linked from `SKILL.md` unless you're blocked.
  - When variants exist (frameworks, providers, domains), pick only the relevant reference file(s) and note that choice.
- Safety and fallback: If a skill can't be applied cleanly (missing files, unclear instructions), state the issue, pick the next-best approach, and continue.

Tool mapping:
- Read: use shell reads (cat/sed) or rg
- Write: create files via shell redirection or apply_patch
- Edit/MultiEdit: use apply_patch
- Bash: use shell_command
- Grep: use rg (fallback: grep)
- Glob: use rg --files or find
- LS: use ls via shell_command
- WebFetch/WebSearch: use curl or Context7 for library docs
- AskUserQuestion/Question: ask the user in chat
- Task/Subagent/Parallel: run sequentially in main thread; use multi_tool_use.parallel for tool calls
- TodoWrite/TodoRead: use file-based todos in todos/ with file-todos skill
- Skill: open the referenced SKILL.md and follow it
- ExitPlanMode: ignore
<!-- END COMPOUND CODEX TOOL MAP -->

# Repository Guidelines

## Project Structure & Module Organization
- `apps/` contains product apps (e.g., `apps/org`, `apps/donor`, `apps/builder`, `apps/storybook`).
- `packages/` contains shared packages like `packages/ui`, `packages/utils`, `packages/tokens`, and config.
- Shared scripts live in `scripts/`, repo config in `config/`, and tooling in `turbo.json` + `biome.jsonc`.

## Build, Test, and Development Commands
- `pnpm dev` starts the local dev experience via `./scripts/dev`.
- `pnpm build` runs `turbo build` for all packages/apps.
- `pnpm test` runs `turbo test` (Vitest in each workspace).
- `pnpm typecheck` runs `turbo typecheck`.
- `pnpm lint` / `pnpm lint:fix` run Biome checks and auto-fixes.
- `pnpm storybook` starts Storybook (`apps/storybook`).
- `pnpm test:storybook` runs Storybook tests.

## Coding Style & Naming Conventions
- Indentation: 2 spaces (see `biome.jsonc`).
- Prefer semantic design tokens and Tailwind utility variables from `packages/ui` (see `CONTRIBUTING.md`).
- File naming: use `kebab-case` for folders, `kebab-case.tsx` for components, and `*.test.ts(x)` for tests.
- Run Biome before pushing: `pnpm lint`.

## Testing Guidelines
- Testing framework: Vitest (see `vitest.config.ts` in apps/packages).
- Name tests as `*.test.ts` or `*.test.tsx` near the source module.
- Run all tests with `pnpm test`, or target a workspace with `pnpm --filter <pkg> test`.

## Commit & Pull Request Guidelines
- Commit messages follow Conventional Commits: `type(scope): subject` (example: `feat(sidebar): add collapse`).
- Commits must be GPG signed (see `CONTRIBUTING.md`).
- Branch naming: `[username]/[feature-description]`.
- PRs should include: concise summary, linked issue (if any), test notes, and screenshots for UI changes.

## Security & Configuration Tips
- Use the setup script for local SSL/hosts setup: `./scripts/setup.sh` (see `SETUP.md`).
- Keep `node` and `pnpm` aligned with `package.json` engines (`node >= 24.6.0`, `pnpm >= 10.6.4`).

### How to use skills
- Discovery: The list above is the skills available in this session (name + description + file path). Skill bodies live on disk at the listed paths.
- Trigger rules: If the user names a skill (with `$SkillName` or plain text) OR the task clearly matches a skill's description shown above, you must use that skill for that turn. Multiple mentions mean use them all. Do not carry skills across turns unless re-mentioned.
- Missing/blocked: If a named skill isn't in the list or the path can't be read, say so briefly and continue with the best fallback.
- How to use a skill (progressive disclosure):
  1) After deciding to use a skill, open its `SKILL.md`. Read only enough to follow the workflow.
  2) When `SKILL.md` references relative paths (e.g., `scripts/foo.py`), resolve them relative to the skill directory listed above first, and only consider other paths if needed.
  3) If `SKILL.md` points to extra folders such as `references/`, load only the specific files needed for the request; don't bulk-load everything.
  4) If `scripts/` exist, prefer running or patching them instead of retyping large code blocks.
  5) If `assets/` or templates exist, reuse them instead of recreating from scratch.
- Coordination and sequencing:
  - If multiple skills apply, choose the minimal set that covers the request and state the order you'll use them.
  - Announce which skill(s) you're using and why (one short line). If you skip an obvious skill, say why.
- Context hygiene:
  - Keep context small: summarize long sections instead of pasting them; only load extra files when needed.
  - Avoid deep reference-chasing: prefer opening only files directly linked from `SKILL.md` unless you're blocked.
  - When variants exist (frameworks, providers, domains), pick only the relevant reference file(s) and note that choice.
- Safety and fallback: If a skill can't be applied cleanly (missing files, unclear instructions), state the issue, pick the next-best approach, and continue.

<!-- BEGIN COMPOUND CODEX TOOL MAP -->
## Compound Codex Tool Mapping (Claude Compatibility)

This section maps Claude Code plugin tool references to Codex behavior.
Only this block is managed automatically.

Tool mapping:
- Read: use shell reads (cat/sed) or rg
- Write: create files via shell redirection or apply_patch
- Edit/MultiEdit: use apply_patch
- Bash: use shell_command
- Grep: use rg (fallback: grep)
- Glob: use rg --files or find
- LS: use ls via shell_command
- WebFetch/WebSearch: use curl or Context7 for library docs
- AskUserQuestion/Question: ask the user in chat
- Task/Subagent/Parallel: run sequentially in main thread; use multi_tool_use.parallel for tool calls
- TodoWrite/TodoRead: use file-based todos in todos/ with file-todos skill
- Skill: open the referenced SKILL.md and follow it
- ExitPlanMode: ignore
<!-- END COMPOUND CODEX TOOL MAP -->

--- project-doc ---

# Repository Guidelines

## Project Structure & Module Organization
- `apps/` contains product apps (e.g., `apps/org`, `apps/donor`, `apps/builder`, `apps/storybook`).
- `packages/` contains shared packages like `packages/ui`, `packages/utils`, `packages/tokens`, and config.
- Shared scripts live in `scripts/`, repo config in `config/`, and tooling in `turbo.json` + `biome.jsonc`.

## Build, Test, and Development Commands
- `pnpm dev` starts the local dev experience via `./scripts/dev`.
- `pnpm build` runs `turbo build` for all packages/apps.
- `pnpm test` runs `turbo test` (Vitest in each workspace).
- `pnpm typecheck` runs `turbo typecheck`.
- `pnpm lint` / `pnpm lint:fix` run Biome checks and auto-fixes.
- `pnpm storybook` starts Storybook (`apps/storybook`).
- `pnpm test:storybook` runs Storybook tests.

## Coding Style & Naming Conventions
- Indentation: 2 spaces (see `biome.jsonc`).
- Prefer semantic design tokens and Tailwind utility variables from `packages/ui` (see `CONTRIBUTING.md`).
- File naming: use `kebab-case` for folders, `kebab-case.tsx` for components, and `*.test.ts(x)` for tests.
- Run Biome before pushing: `pnpm lint`.

## Testing Guidelines
- Testing framework: Vitest (see `vitest.config.ts` in apps/packages).
- Name tests as `*.test.ts` or `*.test.tsx` near the source module.
- Run all tests with `pnpm test`, or target a workspace with `pnpm --filter <pkg> test`.

## Commit & Pull Request Guidelines
- Commit messages follow Conventional Commits: `type(scope): subject` (example: `feat(sidebar): add collapse`).
- Commits must be GPG signed (see `CONTRIBUTING.md`).
- Branch naming: `[username]/[feature-description]`.
- PRs should include: concise summary, linked issue (if any), test notes, and screenshots for UI changes.

## Security & Configuration Tips
- Use the setup script for local SSL/hosts setup: `./scripts/setup.sh` (see `SETUP.md`).
- Keep `node` and `pnpm` aligned with `package.json` engines (`node >= 24.6.0`, `pnpm >= 10.6.4`).


## Skills
A skill is a set of local instructions to follow that is stored in a `SKILL.md` file. Below is the list of skills that can be used. Each entry includes a name, description, and file path so you can open the source for full instructions when using a specific skill.

### How to use skills
- Discovery: The list above is the skills available in this session (name + description + file path). Skill bodies live on disk at the listed paths.
- Trigger rules: If the user names a skill (with `$SkillName` or plain text) OR the task clearly matches a skill's description shown above, you must use that skill for that turn. Multiple mentions mean use them all. Do not carry skills across turns unless re-mentioned.
- Missing/blocked: If a named skill isn't in the list or the path can't be read, say so briefly and continue with the best fallback.
- How to use a skill (progressive disclosure):
  1) After deciding to use a skill, open its `SKILL.md`. Read only enough to follow the workflow.
  2) When `SKILL.md` references relative paths (e.g., `scripts/foo.py`), resolve them relative to the skill directory listed above first, and only consider other paths if needed.
  3) If `SKILL.md` points to extra folders such as `references/`, load only the specific files needed for the request; don't bulk-load everything.
  4) If `scripts/` exist, prefer running or patching them instead of retyping large code blocks.
  5) If `assets/` or templates exist, reuse them instead of recreating from scratch.
- Coordination and sequencing:
  - If multiple skills apply, choose the minimal set that covers the request and state the order you'll use them.
  - Announce which skill(s) you're using and why (one short line). If you skip an obvious skill, say why.
- Context hygiene:
  - Keep context small: summarize long sections instead of pasting them; only load extra files when needed.
  - Avoid deep reference-chasing: prefer opening only files directly linked from `SKILL.md` unless you're blocked.
  - When variants exist (frameworks, providers, domains), pick only the relevant reference file(s) and note that choice.
- Safety and fallback: If a skill can't be applied cleanly (missing files, unclear instructions), state the issue, pick the next-best approach, and continue.

payload.user_instructions:
<!-- BEGIN COMPOUND CODEX TOOL MAP -->
## Compound Codex Tool Mapping (Claude Compatibility)

This section maps Claude Code plugin tool references to Codex behavior.
Only this block is managed automatically.

Tool mapping:
- Read: use shell reads (cat/sed) or rg
- Write: create files via shell redirection or apply_patch
- Edit/MultiEdit: use apply_patch
- Bash: use shell_command
- Grep: use rg (fallback: grep)
- Glob: use rg --files or find
- LS: use ls via shell_command
- WebFetch/WebSearch: use curl or Context7 for library docs
- AskUserQuestion/Question: ask the user in chat
- Task/Subagent/Parallel: run sequentially in main thread; use multi_tool_use.parallel for tool calls
- TodoWrite/TodoRead: use file-based todos in todos/ with file-todos skill
- Skill: open the referenced SKILL.md and follow it
- ExitPlanMode: ignore
<!-- END COMPOUND CODEX TOOL MAP -->

--- project-doc ---

# Repository Guidelines

## Project Structure & Module Organization
- `apps/` contains product apps (e.g., `apps/org`, `apps/donor`, `apps/builder`, `apps/storybook`).
- `packages/` contains shared packages like `packages/ui`, `packages/utils`, `packages/tokens`, and config.
- Shared scripts live in `scripts/`, repo config in `config/`, and tooling in `turbo.json` + `biome.jsonc`.

## Build, Test, and Development Commands
- `pnpm dev` starts the local dev experience via `./scripts/dev`.
- `pnpm build` runs `turbo build` for all packages/apps.
- `pnpm test` runs `turbo test` (Vitest in each workspace).
- `pnpm typecheck` runs `turbo typecheck`.
- `pnpm lint` / `pnpm lint:fix` run Biome checks and auto-fixes.
- `pnpm storybook` starts Storybook (`apps/storybook`).
- `pnpm test:storybook` runs Storybook tests.

## Coding Style & Naming Conventions
- Indentation: 2 spaces (see `biome.jsonc`).
- Prefer semantic design tokens and Tailwind utility variables from `packages/ui` (see `CONTRIBUTING.md`).
- File naming: use `kebab-case` for folders, `kebab-case.tsx` for components, and `*.test.ts(x)` for tests.
- Run Biome before pushing: `pnpm lint`.

## Testing Guidelines
- Testing framework: Vitest (see `vitest.config.ts` in apps/packages).
- Name tests as `*.test.ts` or `*.test.tsx` near the source module.
- Run all tests with `pnpm test`, or target a workspace with `pnpm --filter <pkg> test`.

## Commit & Pull Request Guidelines
- Commit messages follow Conventional Commits: `type(scope): subject` (example: `feat(sidebar): add collapse`).
- Commits must be GPG signed (see `CONTRIBUTING.md`).
- Branch naming: `[username]/[feature-description]`.
- PRs should include: concise summary, linked issue (if any), test notes, and screenshots for UI changes.

## Security & Configuration Tips
- Use the setup script for local SSL/hosts setup: `./scripts/setup.sh` (see `SETUP.md`).
- Keep `node` and `pnpm` aligned with `package.json` engines (`node >= 24.6.0`, `pnpm >= 10.6.4`).


## Skills
A skill is a set of local instructions to follow that is stored in a `SKILL.md` file. Below is the list of skills that can be used. Each entry includes a name, description, and file path so you can open the source for full instructions when using a specific skill.
### Available skills
- agent-browser: Browser automation using Vercel's agent-browser CLI. Use when you need to interact with web pages, fill forms, take screenshots, or scrape data. Alternative to Playwright MCP - uses Bash commands with ref-based element selection. Triggers on "browse website", "fill form", "click button", "take screenshot", "scrape page", "web automation". (file: /Users/duhl/.codex/skills/agent-browser/SKILL.md)

Use this skill when designing autonomous agents, creating MCP tools, implementing self-modifying systems, or building apps where features are outcomes achieved by agents operating in a loop. This enforces the principle that agents should have parity with users in capability and context. <example>Context: The user added a new feature to their application.\nuser: "I just implemented a new email filtering feature"\nassistant: "I'll use the agent-native-reviewer to verify this feature is accessible to agents"\n<commentary>New features need agent-native review to ensure agents can also filter emails, not just humans through UI.</commentary></example><example>Context: The user created a new UI workflow.\nuser: "I added a multi-step wizard for creating reports"\nassistant: "Let me check if this workflow is agent-native using the agent-native-reviewer"\n<commentary>UI workflows often miss agent accessibility - the reviewer checks for API/tool equivalents.</commentary></example> (file: /Users/duhl/.codex/skills/agent-native-reviewer/SKILL.md)

<!-- BEGIN COMPOUND CODEX TOOL MAP -->
## Compound Codex Tool Mapping (Claude Compatibility)

This section maps Claude Code plugin tool references to Codex behavior.
Only this block is managed automatically.

Tool mapping:
- Read: use shell reads (cat/sed) or rg
- Write: create files via shell redirection or apply_patch
- Edit/MultiEdit: use apply_patch
- Bash: use shell_command
- Grep: use rg (fallback: grep)
- Glob: use rg --files or find
- LS: use ls via shell_command
- WebFetch/WebSearch: use curl or Context7 for library docs
- AskUserQuestion/Question: ask the user in chat
- Task/Subagent/Parallel: run sequentially in main thread; use multi_tool_use.parallel for tool calls
- TodoWrite/TodoRead: use file-based todos in todos/ with file-todos skill
- Skill: open the referenced SKILL.md and follow it
- ExitPlanMode: ignore
<!-- END COMPOUND CODEX TOOL MAP -->

--- project-doc ---

# Repository Guidelines

## Project Structure & Module Organization
- `apps/` contains product apps (e.g., `apps/org`, `apps/donor`, `apps/builder`, `apps/storybook`).
- `packages/` contains shared packages like `packages/ui`, `packages/utils`, `packages/tokens`, and config.
- Shared scripts live in `scripts/`, repo config in `config/`, and tooling in `turbo.json` + `biome.jsonc`.

## Build, Test, and Development Commands
- `pnpm dev` starts the local dev experience via `./scripts/dev`.
- `pnpm build` runs `turbo build` for all packages/apps.
- `pnpm test` runs `turbo test` (Vitest in each workspace).
- `pnpm typecheck` runs `turbo typecheck`.
- `pnpm lint` / `pnpm lint:fix` run Biome checks and auto-fixes.
- `pnpm storybook` starts Storybook (`apps/storybook`).
- `pnpm test:storybook` runs Storybook tests.

## Coding Style & Naming Conventions
- Indentation: 2 spaces (see `biome.jsonc`).
- Prefer semantic design tokens and Tailwind utility variables from `packages/ui` (see `CONTRIBUTING.md`).
- File naming: use `kebab-case` for folders, `kebab-case.tsx` for components, and `*.test.ts(x)` for tests.
- Run Biome before pushing: `pnpm lint`.

## Testing Guidelines
- Testing framework: Vitest (see `vitest.config.ts` in apps/packages).
- Name tests as `*.test.ts` or `*.test.tsx` near the source module.
- Run all tests with `pnpm test`, or target a workspace with `pnpm --filter <pkg> test`.

## Commit & Pull Request Guidelines
- Commit messages follow Conventional Commits: `type(scope): subject` (example: `feat(sidebar): add collapse`).
- Commits must be GPG signed (see `CONTRIBUTING.md`).
- Branch naming: `[username]/[feature-description]`.
- PRs should include: concise summary, linked issue (if any), test notes, and screenshots for UI changes.

## Security & Configuration Tips
- Use the setup script for local SSL/hosts setup: `./scripts/setup.sh` (see `SETUP.md`).
- Keep `node` and `pnpm` aligned with `package.json` engines (`node >= 24.6.0`, `pnpm >= 10.6.4`).


## Skills
A skill is a set of local instructions to follow that is stored in a `SKILL.md` file. Below is the list of skills that can be used. Each entry includes a name, description, and file path so you can open the source for full instructions when using a specific skill.

### How to use skills
- Discovery: The list above is the skills available in this session (name + description + file path). Skill bodies live on disk at the listed paths.
- Trigger rules: If the user names a skill (with `$SkillName` or plain text) OR the task clearly matches a skill's description shown above, you must use that skill for that turn. Multiple mentions mean use them all. Do not carry skills across turns unless re-mentioned.
- Missing/blocked: If a named skill isn't in the list or the path can't be read, say so briefly and continue with the best fallback.
- How to use a skill (progressive disclosure):
  1) After deciding to use a skill, open its `SKILL.md`. Read only enough to follow the workflow.
  2) When `SKILL.md` references relative paths (e.g., `scripts/foo.py`), resolve them relative to the skill directory listed above first, and only consider other paths if needed.
  3) If `SKILL.md` points to extra folders such as `references/`, load only the specific files needed for the request; don't bulk-load everything.
  4) If `scripts/` exist, prefer running or patching them instead of retyping large code blocks.
  5) If `assets/` or templates exist, reuse them instead of recreating from scratch.
- Coordination and sequencing:
  - If multiple skills apply, choose the minimal set that covers the request and state the order you'll use them.
  - Announce which skill(s) you're using and why (one short line). If you skip an obvious skill, say why.
- Context hygiene:
  - Keep context small: summarize long sections instead of pasting them; only load extra files when needed.
  - Avoid deep reference-chasing: prefer opening only files directly linked from `SKILL.md` unless you're blocked.
  - When variants exist (frameworks, providers, domains), pick only the relevant reference file(s) and note that choice.
- Safety and fallback: If a skill can't be applied cleanly (missing files, unclear instructions), state the issue, pick the next-best approach, and continue.

<!-- BEGIN COMPOUND CODEX TOOL MAP -->
## Compound Codex Tool Mapping (Claude Compatibility)

This section maps Claude Code plugin tool references to Codex behavior.
Only this block is managed automatically.

Tool mapping:
- Read: use shell reads (cat/sed) or rg
- Write: create files via shell redirection or apply_patch
- Edit/MultiEdit: use apply_patch
- Bash: use shell_command
- Grep: use rg (fallback: grep)
- Glob: use rg --files or find
- LS: use ls via shell_command
- WebFetch/WebSearch: use curl or Context7 for library docs
- AskUserQuestion/Question: ask the user in chat
- Task/Subagent/Parallel: run sequentially in main thread; use multi_tool_use.parallel for tool calls
- TodoWrite/TodoRead: use file-based todos in todos/ with file-todos skill
- Skill: open the referenced SKILL.md and follow it
- ExitPlanMode: ignore
<!-- END COMPOUND CODEX TOOL MAP -->

--- project-doc ---

# Repository Guidelines

## Project Structure & Module Organization
- `apps/` contains product apps (e.g., `apps/org`, `apps/donor`, `apps/builder`, `apps/storybook`).
- `packages/` contains shared packages like `packages/ui`, `packages/utils`, `packages/tokens`, and config.
- Shared scripts live in `scripts/`, repo config in `config/`, and tooling in `turbo.json` + `biome.jsonc`.

## Build, Test, and Development Commands
- `pnpm dev` starts the local dev experience via `./scripts/dev`.
- `pnpm build` runs `turbo build` for all packages/apps.
- `pnpm test` runs `turbo test` (Vitest in each workspace).
- `pnpm typecheck` runs `turbo typecheck`.
- `pnpm lint` / `pnpm lint:fix` run Biome checks and auto-fixes.
- `pnpm storybook` starts Storybook (`apps/storybook`).
- `pnpm test:storybook` runs Storybook tests.

## Coding Style & Naming Conventions
- Indentation: 2 spaces (see `biome.jsonc`).
- Prefer semantic design tokens and Tailwind utility variables from `packages/ui` (see `CONTRIBUTING.md`).
- File naming: use `kebab-case` for folders, `kebab-case.tsx` for components, and `*.test.ts(x)` for tests.
- Run Biome before pushing: `pnpm lint`.

## Testing Guidelines
- Testing framework: Vitest (see `vitest.config.ts` in apps/packages).
- Name tests as `*.test.ts` or `*.test.tsx` near the source module.
- Run all tests with `pnpm test`, or target a workspace with `pnpm --filter <pkg> test`.

## Commit & Pull Request Guidelines
- Commit messages follow Conventional Commits: `type(scope): subject` (example: `feat(sidebar): add collapse`).
- Commits must be GPG signed (see `CONTRIBUTING.md`).
- Branch naming: `[username]/[feature-description]`.
- PRs should include: concise summary, linked issue (if any), test notes, and screenshots for UI changes.

## Security & Configuration Tips
- Use the setup script for local SSL/hosts setup: `./scripts/setup.sh` (see `SETUP.md`).
- Keep `node` and `pnpm` aligned with `package.json` engines (`node >= 24.6.0`, `pnpm >= 10.6.4`).


## Skills
A skill is a set of local instructions to follow that is stored in a `SKILL.md` file. Below is the list of skills that can be used. Each entry includes a name, description, and file path so you can open the source for full instructions when using a specific skill.

Alternative to Playwright MCP - uses Bash commands with ref-based element selection. Triggers on "browse website", "fill form", "click button", "take screenshot", "scrape page", "web automation". ### How to use skills
- Discovery: The list above is the skills available in this session (name + description + file path). Skill bodies live on disk at the listed paths.
- Trigger rules: If the user names a skill (with `$SkillName` or plain text) OR the task clearly matches a skill's description shown above, you must use that skill for that turn. Multiple mentions mean use them all. Do not carry skills across turns unless re-mentioned.
- Missing/blocked: If a named skill isn't in the list or the path can't be read, say so briefly and continue with the best fallback.
- How to use a skill (progressive disclosure):
  1) After deciding to use a skill, open its `SKILL.md`. Read only enough to follow the workflow.
  2) When `SKILL.md` references relative paths (e.g., `scripts/foo.py`), resolve them relative to the skill directory listed above first, and only consider other paths if needed.
  3) If `SKILL.md` points to extra folders such as `references/`, load only the specific files needed for the request; don't bulk-load everything.
  4) If `scripts/` exist, prefer running or patching them instead of retyping large code blocks.
  5) If `assets/` or templates exist, reuse them instead of recreating from scratch.
- Coordination and sequencing:
  - If multiple skills apply, choose the minimal set that covers the request and state the order you'll use them.
  - Announce which skill(s) you're using and why (one short line). If you skip an obvious skill, say why.
- Context hygiene:
  - Keep context small: summarize long sections instead of pasting them; only load extra files when needed.
  - Avoid deep reference-chasing: prefer opening only files directly linked from `SKILL.md` unless you're blocked.
  - When variants exist (frameworks, providers, domains), pick only the relevant reference file(s) and note that choice.
- Safety and fallback: If a skill can't be applied cleanly (missing files, unclear instructions), state the issue, pick the next-best approach, and continue.

## Compound Codex Tool Mapping (Claude Compatibility)

This section maps Claude Code plugin tool references to Codex behavior.
Only this block is managed automatically.

Tool mapping:
- Read: use shell reads (cat/sed) or rg
- Write: create files via shell redirection or apply_patch
- Edit/MultiEdit: use apply_patch
- Bash: use shell_command
- Grep: use rg (fallback: grep)
- Glob: use rg --files or find
- LS: use ls via shell_command
- WebFetch/WebSearch: use curl or Context7 for library docs
- AskUserQuestion/Question: ask the user in chat
- Task/Subagent/Parallel: run sequentially in main thread; use multi_tool_use.parallel for tool calls
- TodoWrite/TodoRead: use file-based todos in todos/ with file-todos skill
- Skill: open the referenced SKILL.md and follow it
- ExitPlanMode: ignore
<!-- END COMPOUND CODEX TOOL MAP -->

--- project-doc ---

# Repository Guidelines

## Project Structure & Module Organization
- `apps/` contains product apps (e.g., `apps/org`, `apps/donor`, `apps/builder`, `apps/storybook`).
- `packages/` contains shared packages like `packages/ui`, `packages/utils`, `packages/tokens`, and config.
- Shared scripts live in `scripts/`, repo config in `config/`, and tooling in `turbo.json` + `biome.jsonc`.

## Build, Test, and Development Commands
- `pnpm dev` starts the local dev experience via `./scripts/dev`.
- `pnpm build` runs `turbo build` for all packages/apps.
- `pnpm test` runs `turbo test` (Vitest in each workspace).
- `pnpm typecheck` runs `turbo typecheck`.
- `pnpm lint` / `pnpm lint:fix` run Biome checks and auto-fixes.
- `pnpm storybook` starts Storybook (`apps/storybook`).
- `pnpm test:storybook` runs Storybook tests.

## Coding Style & Naming Conventions
- Indentation: 2 spaces (see `biome.jsonc`).
- Prefer semantic design tokens and Tailwind utility variables from `packages/ui` (see `CONTRIBUTING.md`).
- File naming: use `kebab-case` for folders, `kebab-case.tsx` for components, and `*.test.ts(x)` for tests.
- Run Biome before pushing: `pnpm lint`.

## Testing Guidelines
- Testing framework: Vitest (see `vitest.config.ts` in apps/packages).
- Name tests as `*.test.ts` or `*.test.tsx` near the source module.
- Run all tests with `pnpm test`, or target a workspace with `pnpm --filter <pkg> test`.

## Commit & Pull Request Guidelines
- Commit messages follow Conventional Commits: `type(scope): subject` (example: `feat(sidebar): add collapse`).
- Commits must be GPG signed (see `CONTRIBUTING.md`).
- Branch naming: `[username]/[feature-description]`.
- PRs should include: concise summary, linked issue (if any), test notes, and screenshots for UI changes.

## Security & Configuration Tips
- Use the setup script for local SSL/hosts setup: `./scripts/setup.sh` (see `SETUP.md`).
- Keep `node` and `pnpm` aligned with `package.json` engines (`node >= 24.6.0`, `pnpm >= 10.6.4`).

### How to use skills
- Discovery: The list above is the skills available in this session (name + description + file path). Skill bodies live on disk at the listed paths.
- Trigger rules: If the user names a skill (with `$SkillName` or plain text) OR the task clearly matches a skill's description shown above, you must use that skill for that turn. Multiple mentions mean use them all. Do not carry skills across turns unless re-mentioned.
- Missing/blocked: If a named skill isn't in the list or the path can't be read, say so briefly and continue with the best fallback.
- How to use a skill (progressive disclosure):
  1) After deciding to use a skill, open its `SKILL.md`. Read only enough to follow the workflow.
  2) When `SKILL.md` references relative paths (e.g., `scripts/foo.py`), resolve them relative to the skill directory listed above first, and only consider other paths if needed.
  3) If `SKILL.md` points to extra folders such as `references/`, load only the specific files needed for the request; don't bulk-load everything.
  4) If `scripts/` exist, prefer running or patching them instead of retyping large code blocks.
  5) If `assets/` or templates exist, reuse them instead of recreating from scratch.
- Coordination and sequencing:
  - If multiple skills apply, choose the minimal set that covers the request and state the order you'll use them.
  - Announce which skill(s) you're using and why (one short line). If you skip an obvious skill, say why.
- Context hygiene:
  - Keep context small: summarize long sections instead of pasting them; only load extra files when needed.
  - Avoid deep reference-chasing: prefer opening only files directly linked from `SKILL.md` unless you're blocked.
  - When variants exist (frameworks, providers, domains), pick only the relevant reference file(s) and note that choice.
- Safety and fallback: If a skill can't be applied cleanly (missing files, unclear instructions), state the issue, pick the next-best approach, and continue.

<!-- BEGIN COMPOUND CODEX TOOL MAP -->
## Compound Codex Tool Mapping (Claude Compatibility)

This section maps Claude Code plugin tool references to Codex behavior.
Only this block is managed automatically.

Tool mapping:
- Read: use shell reads (cat/sed) or rg
- Write: create files via shell redirection or apply_patch
- Edit/MultiEdit: use apply_patch
- Bash: use shell_command
- Grep: use rg (fallback: grep)
- Glob: use rg --files or find
- LS: use ls via shell_command
- WebFetch/WebSearch: use curl or Context7 for library docs
- AskUserQuestion/Question: ask the user in chat
- Task/Subagent/Parallel: run sequentially in main thread; use multi_tool_use.parallel for tool calls
- TodoWrite/TodoRead: use file-based todos in todos/ with file-todos skill
- Skill: open the referenced SKILL.md and follow it
- ExitPlanMode: ignore
<!-- END COMPOUND CODEX TOOL MAP -->

--- project-doc ---

# Repository Guidelines

## Project Structure & Module Organization
- `apps/` contains product apps (e.g., `apps/org`, `apps/donor`, `apps/builder`, `apps/storybook`).
- `packages/` contains shared packages like `packages/ui`, `packages/utils`, `packages/tokens`, and config.
- Shared scripts live in `scripts/`, repo config in `config/`, and tooling in `turbo.json` + `biome.jsonc`.

## Build, Test, and Development Commands
- `pnpm dev` starts the local dev experience via `./scripts/dev`.
- `pnpm build` runs `turbo build` for all packages/apps.
- `pnpm test` runs `turbo test` (Vitest in each workspace).
- `pnpm typecheck` runs `turbo typecheck`.
- `pnpm lint` / `pnpm lint:fix` run Biome checks and auto-fixes.
- `pnpm storybook` starts Storybook (`apps/storybook`).
- `pnpm test:storybook` runs Storybook tests.

## Coding Style & Naming Conventions
- Indentation: 2 spaces (see `biome.jsonc`).
- Prefer semantic design tokens and Tailwind utility variables from `packages/ui` (see `CONTRIBUTING.md`).
- File naming: use `kebab-case` for folders, `kebab-case.tsx` for components, and `*.test.ts(x)` for tests.
- Run Biome before pushing: `pnpm lint`.

## Testing Guidelines
- Testing framework: Vitest (see `vitest.config.ts` in apps/packages).
- Name tests as `*.test.ts` or `*.test.tsx` near the source module.
- Run all tests with `pnpm test`, or target a workspace with `pnpm --filter <pkg> test`.

## Commit & Pull Request Guidelines
- Commit messages follow Conventional Commits: `type(scope): subject` (example: `feat(sidebar): add collapse`).
- Commits must be GPG signed (see `CONTRIBUTING.md`).
- Branch naming: `[username]/[feature-description]`.
- PRs should include: concise summary, linked issue (if any), test notes, and screenshots for UI changes.

## Security & Configuration Tips
- Use the setup script for local SSL/hosts setup: `./scripts/setup.sh` (see `SETUP.md`).
- Keep `node` and `pnpm` aligned with `package.json` engines (`node >= 24.6.0`, `pnpm >= 10.6.4`).


## Skills
A skill is a set of local instructions to follow that is stored in a `SKILL.md` file. Below is the list of skills that can be used. Each entry includes a name, description, and file path so you can open the source for full instructions when using a specific skill.

Alternative to Playwright MCP - uses Bash commands with ref-based element selection. Triggers on "browse website", "fill form", "click button", "take screenshot", "scrape page", "web automation". ### How to use skills
- Discovery: The list above is the skills available in this session (name + description + file path). Skill bodies live on disk at the listed paths.
- Trigger rules: If the user names a skill (with `$SkillName` or plain text) OR the task clearly matches a skill's description shown above, you must use that skill for that turn. Multiple mentions mean use them all. Do not carry skills across turns unless re-mentioned.
- Missing/blocked: If a named skill isn't in the list or the path can't be read, say so briefly and continue with the best fallback.
- How to use a skill (progressive disclosure):
  1) After deciding to use a skill, open its `SKILL.md`. Read only enough to follow the workflow.
  2) When `SKILL.md` references relative paths (e.g., `scripts/foo.py`), resolve them relative to the skill directory listed above first, and only consider other paths if needed.
  3) If `SKILL.md` points to extra folders such as `references/`, load only the specific files needed for the request; don't bulk-load everything.
  4) If `scripts/` exist, prefer running or patching them instead of retyping large code blocks.
  5) If `assets/` or templates exist, reuse them instead of recreating from scratch.
- Coordination and sequencing:
  - If multiple skills apply, choose the minimal set that covers the request and state the order you'll use them.
  - Announce which skill(s) you're using and why (one short line). If you skip an obvious skill, say why.
- Context hygiene:
  - Keep context small: summarize long sections instead of pasting them; only load extra files when needed.
  - Avoid deep reference-chasing: prefer opening only files directly linked from `SKILL.md` unless you're blocked.
  - When variants exist (frameworks, providers, domains), pick only the relevant reference file(s) and note that choice.
- Safety and fallback: If a skill can't be applied cleanly (missing files, unclear instructions), state the issue, pick the next-best approach, and continue.

<!-- BEGIN COMPOUND CODEX TOOL MAP -->
## Compound Codex Tool Mapping (Claude Compatibility)

This section maps Claude Code plugin tool references to Codex behavior.
Only this block is managed automatically.

Tool mapping:
- Read: use shell reads (cat/sed) or rg
- Write: create files via shell redirection or apply_patch
- Edit/MultiEdit: use apply_patch
- Bash: use shell_command
- Grep: use rg (fallback: grep)
- Glob: use rg --files or find
- LS: use ls via shell_command
- WebFetch/WebSearch: use curl or Context7 for library docs
- AskUserQuestion/Question: ask the user in chat
- Task/Subagent/Parallel: run sequentially in main thread; use multi_tool_use.parallel for tool calls
- TodoWrite/TodoRead: use file-based todos in todos/ with file-todos skill
- Skill: open the referenced SKILL.md and follow it
- ExitPlanMode: ignore
<!-- END COMPOUND CODEX TOOL MAP -->

--- project-doc ---

# Repository Guidelines

## Project Structure & Module Organization
- `apps/` contains product apps (e.g., `apps/org`, `apps/donor`, `apps/builder`, `apps/storybook`).
- `packages/` contains shared packages like `packages/ui`, `packages/utils`, `packages/tokens`, and config.
- Shared scripts live in `scripts/`, repo config in `config/`, and tooling in `turbo.json` + `biome.jsonc`.

## Build, Test, and Development Commands
- `pnpm dev` starts the local dev experience via `./scripts/dev`.
- `pnpm build` runs `turbo build` for all packages/apps.
- `pnpm test` runs `turbo test` (Vitest in each workspace).
- `pnpm typecheck` runs `turbo typecheck`.
- `pnpm lint` / `pnpm lint:fix` run Biome checks and auto-fixes.
- `pnpm storybook` starts Storybook (`apps/storybook`).
- `pnpm test:storybook` runs Storybook tests.

## Coding Style & Naming Conventions
- Indentation: 2 spaces (see `biome.jsonc`).
- Prefer semantic design tokens and Tailwind utility variables from `packages/ui` (see `CONTRIBUTING.md`).
- File naming: use `kebab-case` for folders, `kebab-case.tsx` for components, and `*.test.ts(x)` for tests.
- Run Biome before pushing: `pnpm lint`.

## Testing Guidelines
- Testing framework: Vitest (see `vitest.config.ts` in apps/packages).
- Name tests as `*.test.ts` or `*.test.tsx` near the source module.
- Run all tests with `pnpm test`, or target a workspace with `pnpm --filter <pkg> test`.

## Commit & Pull Request Guidelines
- Commit messages follow Conventional Commits: `type(scope): subject` (example: `feat(sidebar): add collapse`).
- Commits must be GPG signed (see `CONTRIBUTING.md`).
- Branch naming: `[username]/[feature-description]`.
- PRs should include: concise summary, linked issue (if any), test notes, and screenshots for UI changes.

## Security & Configuration Tips
- Use the setup script for local SSL/hosts setup: `./scripts/setup.sh` (see `SETUP.md`).
- Keep `node` and `pnpm` aligned with `package.json` engines (`node >= 24.6.0`, `pnpm >= 10.6.4`).


## Skills
A skill is a set of local instructions to follow that is stored in a `SKILL.md` file. Below is the list of skills that can be used. Each entry includes a name, description, and file path so you can open the source for full instructions when using a specific skill.
### Available skills
- agent-browser: Browser automation using Vercel's agent-browser CLI. Use when you need to interact with web pages, fill forms, take screenshots, or scrape data. Alternative to Playwright MCP - uses Bash commands with ref-based element selection. Triggers on "browse website", "fill form", "click button", "take screenshot", "scrape page", "web automation". (file: /Users/duhl/.codex/skills/agent-browser/SKILL.md)

Use this skill when designing autonomous agents, creating MCP tools, implementing self-modifying systems, or building apps where features are outcomes achieved by agents operating in a loop. <!-- BEGIN COMPOUND CODEX TOOL MAP -->
## Compound Codex Tool Mapping (Claude Compatibility)

This section maps Claude Code plugin tool references to Codex behavior.
Only this block is managed automatically.

Tool mapping:
- Read: use shell reads (cat/sed) or rg
- Write: create files via shell redirection or apply_patch
- Edit/MultiEdit: use apply_patch
- Bash: use shell_command
- Grep: use rg (fallback: grep)
- Glob: use rg --files or find
- LS: use ls via shell_command
- WebFetch/WebSearch: use curl or Context7 for library docs
- AskUserQuestion/Question: ask the user in chat
- Task/Subagent/Parallel: run sequentially in main thread; use multi_tool_use.parallel for tool calls
- TodoWrite/TodoRead: use file-based todos in todos/ with file-todos skill
- Skill: open the referenced SKILL.md and follow it
- ExitPlanMode: ignore
<!-- END COMPOUND CODEX TOOL MAP -->

--- project-doc ---

# Repository Guidelines

## Project Structure & Module Organization
- `apps/` contains product apps (e.g., `apps/org`, `apps/donor`, `apps/builder`, `apps/storybook`).
- `packages/` contains shared packages like `packages/ui`, `packages/utils`, `packages/tokens`, and config.
- Shared scripts live in `scripts/`, repo config in `config/`, and tooling in `turbo.json` + `biome.jsonc`.

## Build, Test, and Development Commands
- `pnpm dev` starts the local dev experience via `./scripts/dev`.
- `pnpm build` runs `turbo build` for all packages/apps.
- `pnpm test` runs `turbo test` (Vitest in each workspace).
- `pnpm typecheck` runs `turbo typecheck`.
- `pnpm lint` / `pnpm lint:fix` run Biome checks and auto-fixes.
- `pnpm storybook` starts Storybook (`apps/storybook`).
- `pnpm test:storybook` runs Storybook tests.

## Coding Style & Naming Conventions
- Indentation: 2 spaces (see `biome.jsonc`).
- Prefer semantic design tokens and Tailwind utility variables from `packages/ui` (see `CONTRIBUTING.md`).
- File naming: use `kebab-case` for folders, `kebab-case.tsx` for components, and `*.test.ts(x)` for tests.
- Run Biome before pushing: `pnpm lint`.

## Testing Guidelines
- Testing framework: Vitest (see `vitest.config.ts` in apps/packages).
- Name tests as `*.test.ts` or `*.test.tsx` near the source module.
- Run all tests with `pnpm test`, or target a workspace with `pnpm --filter <pkg> test`.

## Commit & Pull Request Guidelines
- Commit messages follow Conventional Commits: `type(scope): subject` (example: `feat(sidebar): add collapse`).
- Commits must be GPG signed (see `CONTRIBUTING.md`).
- Branch naming: `[username]/[feature-description]`.
- PRs should include: concise summary, linked issue (if any), test notes, and screenshots for UI changes.

## Security & Configuration Tips
- Use the setup script for local SSL/hosts setup: `./scripts/setup.sh` (see `SETUP.md`).
- Keep `node` and `pnpm` aligned with `package.json` engines (`node >= 24.6.0`, `pnpm >= 10.6.4`).


## Skills
A skill is a set of local instructions to follow that is stored in a `SKILL.md` file. Below is the list of skills that can be used. Each entry includes a name, description, and file path so you can open the source for full instructions when using a specific skill.

- Discovery: The list above is the skills available in this session (name + description + file path). Skill bodies live on disk at the listed paths.

- How to use a skill (progressive disclosure):
  1) After deciding to use a skill, open its `SKILL.md`. Read only enough to follow the workflow.
  2) When `SKILL.md` references relative paths (e.g., `scripts/foo.py`), resolve them relative to the skill directory listed above first, and only consider other paths if needed.

- Coordination and sequencing:

- Context hygiene:

  - Avoid deep reference-chasing: prefer opening only files directly linked from `SKILL.md` unless you're blocked.
  - When variants exist (frameworks, providers, domains), pick only the relevant reference file(s) and note that choice.
- Safety and fallback: If a skill can't be applied cleanly (missing files, unclear instructions), state the issue, pick the next-best approach, and continue.

<!-- BEGIN COMPOUND CODEX TOOL MAP -->
## Compound Codex Tool Mapping (Claude Compatibility)

This section maps Claude Code plugin tool references to Codex behavior.
Only this block is managed automatically.

Tool mapping:
- Read: use shell reads (cat/sed) or rg
- Write: create files via shell redirection or apply_patch
- Edit/MultiEdit: use apply_patch
- Bash: use shell_command
- Grep: use rg (fallback: grep)
- Glob: use rg --files or find
- LS: use ls via shell_command
- WebFetch/WebSearch: use curl or Context7 for library docs
- AskUserQuestion/Question: ask the user in chat
- Task/Subagent/Parallel: run sequentially in main thread; use multi_tool_use.parallel for tool calls
- TodoWrite/TodoRead: use file-based todos in todos/ with file-todos skill
- Skill: open the referenced SKILL.md and follow it
- ExitPlanMode: ignore
<!-- END COMPOUND CODEX TOOL MAP -->

--- project-doc ---

# Repository Guidelines

## Project Structure & Module Organization
- `apps/` contains product apps (e.g., `apps/org`, `apps/donor`, `apps/builder`, `apps/storybook`).
- `packages/` contains shared packages like `packages/ui`, `packages/utils`, `packages/tokens`, and config.
- Shared scripts live in `scripts/`, repo config in `config/`, and tooling in `turbo.json` + `biome.jsonc`.

## Build, Test, and Development Commands
- `pnpm dev` starts the local dev experience via `./scripts/dev`.
- `pnpm build` runs `turbo build` for all packages/apps.
- `pnpm test` runs `turbo test` (Vitest in each workspace).
- `pnpm typecheck` runs `turbo typecheck`.
- `pnpm lint` / `pnpm lint:fix` run Biome checks and auto-fixes.
- `pnpm storybook` starts Storybook (`apps/storybook`).
- `pnpm test:storybook` runs Storybook tests.

## Coding Style & Naming Conventions
- Indentation: 2 spaces (see `biome.jsonc`).
- Prefer semantic design tokens and Tailwind utility variables from `packages/ui` (see `CONTRIBUTING.md`).
- File naming: use `kebab-case` for folders, `kebab-case.tsx` for components, and `*.test.ts(x)` for tests.
- Run Biome before pushing: `pnpm lint`.

## Testing Guidelines
- Testing framework: Vitest (see `vitest.config.ts` in apps/packages).
- Name tests as `*.test.ts` or `*.test.tsx` near the source module.
- Run all tests with `pnpm test`, or target a workspace with `pnpm --filter <pkg> test`.

## Commit & Pull Request Guidelines
- Commit messages follow Conventional Commits: `type(scope): subject` (example: `feat(sidebar): add collapse`).
- Commits must be GPG signed (see `CONTRIBUTING.md`).
- Branch naming: `[username]/[feature-description]`.
- PRs should include: concise summary, linked issue (if any), test notes, and screenshots for UI changes.

## Security & Configuration Tips
- Use the setup script for local SSL/hosts setup: `./scripts/setup.sh` (see `SETUP.md`).
- Keep `node` and `pnpm` aligned with `package.json` engines (`node >= 24.6.0`, `pnpm >= 10.6.4`).


## Skills
A skill is a set of local instructions to follow that is stored in a `SKILL.md` file. Below is the list of skills that can be used. Each entry includes a name, description, and file path so you can open the source for full instructions when using a specific skill.

Alternative to Playwright MCP - uses Bash commands with ref-based element selection. Triggers on "browse website", "fill form", "click button", "take screenshot", "scrape page", "web automation". This section maps Claude Code plugin tool references to Codex behavior.
Only this block is managed automatically.

--- project-doc ---

# Repository Guidelines

## Project Structure & Module Organization
- `apps/` contains product apps (e.g., `apps/org`, `apps/donor`, `apps/builder`, `apps/storybook`).
- `packages/` contains shared packages like `packages/ui`, `packages/utils`, `packages/tokens`, and config.
- Shared scripts live in `scripts/`, repo config in `config/`, and tooling in `turbo.json` + `biome.jsonc`.

## Build, Test, and Development Commands
- `pnpm dev` starts the local dev experience via `./scripts/dev`.
- `pnpm build` runs `turbo build` for all packages/apps.
- `pnpm test` runs `turbo test` (Vitest in each workspace).
- `pnpm typecheck` runs `turbo typecheck`.
- `pnpm lint` / `pnpm lint:fix` run Biome checks and auto-fixes.
- `pnpm storybook` starts Storybook (`apps/storybook`).
- `pnpm test:storybook` runs Storybook tests.

## Coding Style & Naming Conventions
- Indentation: 2 spaces (see `biome.jsonc`).
- Prefer semantic design tokens and Tailwind utility variables from `packages/ui` (see `CONTRIBUTING.md`).
- File naming: use `kebab-case` for folders, `kebab-case.tsx` for components, and `*.test.ts(x)` for tests.
- Run Biome before pushing: `pnpm lint`.

## Testing Guidelines
- Testing framework: Vitest (see `vitest.config.ts` in apps/packages).
- Name tests as `*.test.ts` or `*.test.tsx` near the source module.
- Run all tests with `pnpm test`, or target a workspace with `pnpm --filter <pkg> test`.

## Commit & Pull Request Guidelines
- Commit messages follow Conventional Commits: `type(scope): subject` (example: `feat(sidebar): add collapse`).
- Commits must be GPG signed (see `CONTRIBUTING.md`).
- Branch naming: `[username]/[feature-description]`.
- PRs should include: concise summary, linked issue (if any), test notes, and screenshots for UI changes.

## Security & Configuration Tips
- Use the setup script for local SSL/hosts setup: `./scripts/setup.sh` (see `SETUP.md`).
- Keep `node` and `pnpm` aligned with `package.json` engines (`node >= 24.6.0`, `pnpm >= 10.6.4`).


## Skills
A skill is a set of local instructions to follow that is stored in a `SKILL.md` file. Below is the list of skills that can be used. Each entry includes a name, description, and file path so you can open the source for full instructions when using a specific skill.

### How to use skills
- Discovery: The list above is the skills available in this session (name + description + file path). Skill bodies live on disk at the listed paths.
- Trigger rules: If the user names a skill (with `$SkillName` or plain text) OR the task clearly matches a skill's description shown above, you must use that skill for that turn. Multiple mentions mean use them all. Do not carry skills across turns unless re-mentioned.
- Missing/blocked: If a named skill isn't in the list or the path can't be read, say so briefly and continue with the best fallback.
- How to use a skill (progressive disclosure):
  1) After deciding to use a skill, open its `SKILL.md`. Read only enough to follow the workflow.
  2) When `SKILL.md` references relative paths (e.g., `scripts/foo.py`), resolve them relative to the skill directory listed above first, and only consider other paths if needed.
  3) If `SKILL.md` points to extra folders such as `references/`, load only the specific files needed for the request; don't bulk-load everything.
  4) If `scripts/` exist, prefer running or patching them instead of retyping large code blocks.
  5) If `assets/` or templates exist, reuse them instead of recreating from scratch.
- Coordination and sequencing:
  - If multiple skills apply, choose the minimal set that covers the request and state the order you'll use them.
  - Announce which skill(s) you're using and why (one short line). If you skip an obvious skill, say why.
- Context hygiene:
  - Keep context small: summarize long sections instead of pasting them; only load extra files when needed.
  - Avoid deep reference-chasing: prefer opening only files directly linked from `SKILL.md` unless you're blocked.
  - When variants exist (frameworks, providers, domains), pick only the relevant reference file(s) and note that choice.
- Safety and fallback: If a skill can't be applied cleanly (missing files, unclear instructions), state the issue, pick the next-best approach, and continue.

<!-- BEGIN COMPOUND CODEX TOOL MAP -->
## Compound Codex Tool Mapping (Claude Compatibility)

This section maps Claude Code plugin tool references to Codex behavior.
Only this block is managed automatically.

Tool mapping:
- Read: use shell reads (cat/sed) or rg
- Write: create files via shell redirection or apply_patch
- Edit/MultiEdit: use apply_patch
- Bash: use shell_command
- Grep: use rg (fallback: grep)
- Glob: use rg --files or find
- LS: use ls via shell_command
- WebFetch/WebSearch: use curl or Context7 for library docs
- AskUserQuestion/Question: ask the user in chat
- Task/Subagent/Parallel: run sequentially in main thread; use multi_tool_use.parallel for tool calls
- TodoWrite/TodoRead: use file-based todos in todos/ with file-todos skill
- Skill: open the referenced SKILL.md and follow it
- ExitPlanMode: ignore
<!-- END COMPOUND CODEX TOOL MAP -->

--- project-doc ---

# Repository Guidelines

## Project Structure & Module Organization
- `apps/` contains product apps (e.g., `apps/org`, `apps/donor`, `apps/builder`, `apps/storybook`).
- `packages/` contains shared packages like `packages/ui`, `packages/utils`, `packages/tokens`, and config.
- Shared scripts live in `scripts/`, repo config in `config/`, and tooling in `turbo.json` + `biome.jsonc`.

## Build, Test, and Development Commands
- `pnpm dev` starts the local dev experience via `./scripts/dev`.
- `pnpm build` runs `turbo build` for all packages/apps.
- `pnpm test` runs `turbo test` (Vitest in each workspace).
- `pnpm typecheck` runs `turbo typecheck`.
- `pnpm lint` / `pnpm lint:fix` run Biome checks and auto-fixes.
- `pnpm storybook` starts Storybook (`apps/storybook`).
- `pnpm test:storybook` runs Storybook tests.

## Coding Style & Naming Conventions
- Indentation: 2 spaces (see `biome.jsonc`).
- Prefer semantic design tokens and Tailwind utility variables from `packages/ui` (see `CONTRIBUTING.md`).
- File naming: use `kebab-case` for folders, `kebab-case.tsx` for components, and `*.test.ts(x)` for tests.
- Run Biome before pushing: `pnpm lint`.

## Testing Guidelines
- Testing framework: Vitest (see `vitest.config.ts` in apps/packages).
- Name tests as `*.test.ts` or `*.test.tsx` near the source module.
- Run all tests with `pnpm test`, or target a workspace with `pnpm --filter <pkg> test`.

## Commit & Pull Request Guidelines
- Commit messages follow Conventional Commits: `type(scope): subject` (example: `feat(sidebar): add collapse`).
- Commits must be GPG signed (see `CONTRIBUTING.md`).
- Branch naming: `[username]/[feature-description]`.
- PRs should include: concise summary, linked issue (if any), test notes, and screenshots for UI changes.

## Security & Configuration Tips
- Use the setup script for local SSL/hosts setup: `./scripts/setup.sh` (see `SETUP.md`).
- Keep `node` and `pnpm` aligned with `package.json` engines (`node >= 24.6.0`, `pnpm >= 10.6.4`).


## Skills
A skill is a set of local instructions to follow that is stored in a `SKILL.md` file. Below is the list of skills that can be used. Each entry includes a name, description, and file path so you can open the source for full instructions when using a specific skill.

Alternative to Playwright MCP - uses Bash commands with ref-based element selection. Triggers on "browse website", "fill form", "click button", "take screenshot", "scrape page", "web automation". ### How to use skills
- Discovery: The list above is the skills available in this session (name + description + file path). Skill bodies live on disk at the listed paths.
- Trigger rules: If the user names a skill (with `$SkillName` or plain text) OR the task clearly matches a skill's description shown above, you must use that skill for that turn. Multiple mentions mean use them all. Do not carry skills across turns unless re-mentioned.
- Missing/blocked: If a named skill isn't in the list or the path can't be read, say so briefly and continue with the best fallback.
- How to use a skill (progressive disclosure):
  1) After deciding to use a skill, open its `SKILL.md`. Read only enough to follow the workflow.
  2) When `SKILL.md` references relative paths (e.g., `scripts/foo.py`), resolve them relative to the skill directory listed above first, and only consider other paths if needed.
  3) If `SKILL.md` points to extra folders such as `references/`, load only the specific files needed for the request; don't bulk-load everything.
  4) If `scripts/` exist, prefer running or patching them instead of retyping large code blocks.
  5) If `assets/` or templates exist, reuse them instead of recreating from scratch.
- Coordination and sequencing:
  - If multiple skills apply, choose the minimal set that covers the request and state the order you'll use them.
  - Announce which skill(s) you're using and why (one short line). If you skip an obvious skill, say why.
- Context hygiene:
  - Keep context small: summarize long sections instead of pasting them; only load extra files when needed.
  - Avoid deep reference-chasing: prefer opening only files directly linked from `SKILL.md` unless you're blocked.
  - When variants exist (frameworks, providers, domains), pick only the relevant reference file(s) and note that choice.
- Safety and fallback: If a skill can't be applied cleanly (missing files, unclear instructions), state the issue, pick the next-best approach, and continue.

payload.text:
**Evaluating Tailwind v4 and pnpm catalog impacts**

Considering potential break from Tailwind v4 changes to PostCSS config in apps, and whether some apps still use Tailwind v3 config causing runtime issues. Also assessing pnpm workspace's strict catalog mode possibly causing install failures if dependency versions aren't properly referenced or cataloged. Next, I'll verify actual errors and dependency declarations against the catalog.

**Identifying catalog mode version mismatches**

Noticed some package.json files use explicit versions while the catalog defines different versions, which may cause install failures in strict catalog mode requiring "catalog:" specs. I'll check all packages for such mismatches to confirm this issue.

## Compound Codex Tool Mapping (Claude Compatibility)

This section maps Claude Code plugin tool references to Codex behavior.
Only this block is managed automatically.

Tool mapping:
- Read: use shell reads (cat/sed) or rg
- Write: create files via shell redirection or apply_patch
- Edit/MultiEdit: use apply_patch
- Bash: use shell_command
- Grep: use rg (fallback: grep)
- Glob: use rg --files or find
- LS: use ls via shell_command
- WebFetch/WebSearch: use curl or Context7 for library docs
- AskUserQuestion/Question: ask the user in chat
- Task/Subagent/Parallel: run sequentially in main thread; use multi_tool_use.parallel for tool calls
- TodoWrite/TodoRead: use file-based todos in todos/ with file-todos skill
- Skill: open the referenced SKILL.md and follow it
- ExitPlanMode: ignore
<!-- END COMPOUND CODEX TOOL MAP -->

--- project-doc ---

# Repository Guidelines

## Project Structure & Module Organization
- `apps/` contains product apps (e.g., `apps/org`, `apps/donor`, `apps/builder`, `apps/storybook`).
- `packages/` contains shared packages like `packages/ui`, `packages/utils`, `packages/tokens`, and config.
- Shared scripts live in `scripts/`, repo config in `config/`, and tooling in `turbo.json` + `biome.jsonc`.

## Build, Test, and Development Commands
- `pnpm dev` starts the local dev experience via `./scripts/dev`.
- `pnpm build` runs `turbo build` for all packages/apps.
- `pnpm test` runs `turbo test` (Vitest in each workspace).
- `pnpm typecheck` runs `turbo typecheck`.
- `pnpm lint` / `pnpm lint:fix` run Biome checks and auto-fixes.
- `pnpm storybook` starts Storybook (`apps/storybook`).
- `pnpm test:storybook` runs Storybook tests.

## Coding Style & Naming Conventions
- Indentation: 2 spaces (see `biome.jsonc`).
- Prefer semantic design tokens and Tailwind utility variables from `packages/ui` (see `CONTRIBUTING.md`).
- File naming: use `kebab-case` for folders, `kebab-case.tsx` for components, and `*.test.ts(x)` for tests.
- Run Biome before pushing: `pnpm lint`.

## Testing Guidelines
- Testing framework: Vitest (see `vitest.config.ts` in apps/packages).
- Name tests as `*.test.ts` or `*.test.tsx` near the source module.
- Run all tests with `pnpm test`, or target a workspace with `pnpm --filter <pkg> test`.

## Commit & Pull Request Guidelines
- Commit messages follow Conventional Commits: `type(scope): subject` (example: `feat(sidebar): add collapse`).
- Commits must be GPG signed (see `CONTRIBUTING.md`).
- Branch naming: `[username]/[feature-description]`.
- PRs should include: concise summary, linked issue (if any), test notes, and screenshots for UI changes.

## Security & Configuration Tips
- Use the setup script for local SSL/hosts setup: `./scripts/setup.sh` (see `SETUP.md`).
- Keep `node` and `pnpm` aligned with `package.json` engines (`node >= 24.6.0`, `pnpm >= 10.6.4`).


## Skills
A skill is a set of local instructions to follow that is stored in a `SKILL.md` file. Below is the list of skills that can be used. Each entry includes a name, description, and file path so you can open the source for full instructions when using a specific skill.

- How to use a skill (progressive disclosure):
  1) After deciding to use a skill, open its `SKILL.md`. Read only enough to follow the workflow.
  2) When `SKILL.md` references relative paths (e.g., `scripts/foo.py`), resolve them relative to the skill directory listed above first, and only consider other paths if needed.
  3) If `SKILL.md` points to extra folders such as `references/`, load only the specific files needed for the request; don't bulk-load everything.

- Safety and fallback: If a skill can't be applied cleanly (missing files, unclear instructions), state the issue, pick the next-best approach, and continue.

**Assessing strict catalog mode impact**

Strict catalog mode requires dependencies to use catalog entries exclusively, causing install failures if explicit versions remain; some packages still use explicit versions despite catalog adoption, so I need to identify all such instances to prevent errors.

**Verifying catalog usage across packages**

Some packages like apps/builder have switched core libs to catalog references, but others like apps/donor may still use explicit versions; next, I’ll check those to ensure consistent catalog usage.

<!-- BEGIN COMPOUND CODEX TOOL MAP -->
## Compound Codex Tool Mapping (Claude Compatibility)

This section maps Claude Code plugin tool references to Codex behavior.
Only this block is managed automatically.

Tool mapping:
- Read: use shell reads (cat/sed) or rg
- Write: create files via shell redirection or apply_patch
- Edit/MultiEdit: use apply_patch
- Bash: use shell_command
- Grep: use rg (fallback: grep)
- Glob: use rg --files or find
- LS: use ls via shell_command
- WebFetch/WebSearch: use curl or Context7 for library docs
- AskUserQuestion/Question: ask the user in chat
- Task/Subagent/Parallel: run sequentially in main thread; use multi_tool_use.parallel for tool calls
- TodoWrite/TodoRead: use file-based todos in todos/ with file-todos skill
- Skill: open the referenced SKILL.md and follow it
- ExitPlanMode: ignore
<!-- END COMPOUND CODEX TOOL MAP -->

--- project-doc ---

# Repository Guidelines

## Project Structure & Module Organization
- `apps/` contains product apps (e.g., `apps/org`, `apps/donor`, `apps/builder`, `apps/storybook`).
- `packages/` contains shared packages like `packages/ui`, `packages/utils`, `packages/tokens`, and config.
- Shared scripts live in `scripts/`, repo config in `config/`, and tooling in `turbo.json` + `biome.jsonc`.

## Build, Test, and Development Commands
- `pnpm dev` starts the local dev experience via `./scripts/dev`.
- `pnpm build` runs `turbo build` for all packages/apps.
- `pnpm test` runs `turbo test` (Vitest in each workspace).
- `pnpm typecheck` runs `turbo typecheck`.
- `pnpm lint` / `pnpm lint:fix` run Biome checks and auto-fixes.
- `pnpm storybook` starts Storybook (`apps/storybook`).
- `pnpm test:storybook` runs Storybook tests.

## Coding Style & Naming Conventions
- Indentation: 2 spaces (see `biome.jsonc`).
- Prefer semantic design tokens and Tailwind utility variables from `packages/ui` (see `CONTRIBUTING.md`).
- File naming: use `kebab-case` for folders, `kebab-case.tsx` for components, and `*.test.ts(x)` for tests.
- Run Biome before pushing: `pnpm lint`.

## Testing Guidelines
- Testing framework: Vitest (see `vitest.config.ts` in apps/packages).
- Name tests as `*.test.ts` or `*.test.tsx` near the source module.
- Run all tests with `pnpm test`, or target a workspace with `pnpm --filter <pkg> test`.

## Commit & Pull Request Guidelines
- Commit messages follow Conventional Commits: `type(scope): subject` (example: `feat(sidebar): add collapse`).
- Commits must be GPG signed (see `CONTRIBUTING.md`).
- Branch naming: `[username]/[feature-description]`.
- PRs should include: concise summary, linked issue (if any), test notes, and screenshots for UI changes.

## Security & Configuration Tips
- Use the setup script for local SSL/hosts setup: `./scripts/setup.sh` (see `SETUP.md`).
- Keep `node` and `pnpm` aligned with `package.json` engines (`node >= 24.6.0`, `pnpm >= 10.6.4`).


## Skills
A skill is a set of local instructions to follow that is stored in a `SKILL.md` file. Below is the list of skills that can be used. Each entry includes a name, description, and file path so you can open the source for full instructions when using a specific skill.

### How to use skills
- Discovery: The list above is the skills available in this session (name + description + file path). Skill bodies live on disk at the listed paths.
- Trigger rules: If the user names a skill (with `$SkillName` or plain text) OR the task clearly matches a skill's description shown above, you must use that skill for that turn. Multiple mentions mean use them all. Do not carry skills across turns unless re-mentioned.
- Missing/blocked: If a named skill isn't in the list or the path can't be read, say so briefly and continue with the best fallback.
- How to use a skill (progressive disclosure):
  1) After deciding to use a skill, open its `SKILL.md`. Read only enough to follow the workflow.
  2) When `SKILL.md` references relative paths (e.g., `scripts/foo.py`), resolve them relative to the skill directory listed above first, and only consider other paths if needed.
  3) If `SKILL.md` points to extra folders such as `references/`, load only the specific files needed for the request; don't bulk-load everything.
  4) If `scripts/` exist, prefer running or patching them instead of retyping large code blocks.
  5) If `assets/` or templates exist, reuse them instead of recreating from scratch.
- Coordination and sequencing:
  - If multiple skills apply, choose the minimal set that covers the request and state the order you'll use them.
  - Announce which skill(s) you're using and why (one short line). If you skip an obvious skill, say why.
- Context hygiene:
  - Keep context small: summarize long sections instead of pasting them; only load extra files when needed.
  - Avoid deep reference-chasing: prefer opening only files directly linked from `SKILL.md` unless you're blocked.
  - When variants exist (frameworks, providers, domains), pick only the relevant reference file(s) and note that choice.
- Safety and fallback: If a skill can't be applied cleanly (missing files, unclear instructions), state the issue, pick the next-best approach, and continue.
